summary: Joel Spolsky mocks Microsoft's 'Ship It' award program, introduced by HR manager Mike Murray, which gave employees a lucite tombstone when their product shipped. Spolsky finds the idea that a physical trophy would motivate software developers to be absurd and patronizing. He uses this as a launching point to direct readers to his longer essay on why incentive pay is counterproductive. The brief post illustrates his broader skepticism of extrinsic motivation schemes in knowledge work.
key_points: Microsoft's 'Ship It' award gave developers a lucite trophy when products shipped, as if that would motivate them to work harder
The absurdity of the incentive is highlighted by noting Microsoft shipped software perfectly well before the award existed
Extrinsic motivators like trophies and incentive pay are misguided approaches to managing knowledge workers
money_quotes: This was somehow supposed to give you an incentive to work, you see, because if you didn't do your job– no lucite for you!
Makes you wonder how Microsoft ever shipped software before the lucite slab.
key_insight: Superficial incentive programs like trophies reveal a fundamental misunderstanding of what actually motivates knowledge workers.
